title: Today's AI Brief: Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop
summary: A new startup from former Google and Apple researchers aims to improve AI systems with a feedback loop, while AI's impact on politics and careers is making headlines.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop
imageCaption: Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop
imageSourceTitle: Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. I'm looking at a couple of interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First up, a group of researchers who used to work at Google DeepMind, Apple, OpenAI, and Meta Superintelligence Labs have launched a new startup called Trajectory. Their goal is to build a feedback loop for AI systems, which essentially means creating AI that gets smarter as you use it.

The idea here is that current AI systems can be a bit one-dimensional. They learn from the data they're trained on, but they don't really get to learn from the people who are using them. Trajectory's founders think that by adding a feedback loop, AI systems can become more accurate, more helpful, and maybe even a bit more intuitive. It's like having a conversation with a friend who actually listens to what you're saying and adjusts their responses accordingly.

In other news, it seems like AI is having a bit of an impact on politics. According to Axios, AI, crypto, and AIPAC are all playing a role in ending political careers. I won't get too into the weeds here, but it's clear that AI is becoming a factor in the world of politics, and that's something to keep an eye on.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
